findings.text: order-service DB connection pool is fully exhausted — active connections hit the configured maximum while connection waiters are growing, indicating requests are queuing for connections that never become available.

CPUUtilization for order-service has been sustained above 90% for an extended period, confirming the service is under severe resource pressure and cannot process requests fast enough to release connections.
The service is running with only 2 desired tasks in ECS, which is insufficient to handle the current load — the infra inspector flagged CPU/memory as undersized for the current load.

The order-service DB client uses a fixed pool size with no adaptive backoff on checkout failure, meaning the service cannot recover gracefully when the pool is saturated — it simply blocks waiting for connections.
No recent deployments or configuration changes in the last 24 hours — the change detector confirms this is a load-driven issue, not a change-driven regression.
No lock contention on primary database tables — the DB analyst confirmed the bottleneck is pool sizing, not query-level contention, so the fix must address the connection layer, not the SQL layer.

Queue depth is rising concurrently with pool saturation, indicating that the service is not only unable to process new requests but is also failing to drain existing work, creating a compounding feedback loop.

body.potentialRootCause: The order-service is experiencing a cascading failure caused by a fixed-size DB connection pool that is fully saturated under elevated load. With only 2 ECS tasks running and no adaptive backoff on connection checkout failures, the service cannot recover when the pool is exhausted. As connections pile up waiting for checkout, CPU rises above 90% due to thread-pool saturation, which further prevents connections from being released back to the pool — creating a positive feedback loop. The root cause is a combination of: (1) undersized task capacity (2 tasks), (2) a rigid connection pool with no backoff/retry strategy, and (3) no circuit-breaking mechanism to shed load when the DB is unreachable. This is a load-driven issue confirmed by the absence of recent deployments.

recommendedActions.label: Scale order-service tasks from 2 to 6
recommendedActions.description: Increase the desired task count for the order-service ECS service from 2 to 6 to immediately increase processing capacity and reduce per-task CPU pressure, allowing connections to be released faster.
recommendedActions.rationale: Infra inspector confirmed only 2 desired tasks are running with undersized CPU/memory. Scaling to 6 tasks will distribute the load, reduce per-task CPU, and allow the connection pool to drain more quickly.

recommendedActions.label: Add idle timeout to order-service DB connections
recommendedActions.description: Configure an idle connection timeout (e.g., 30 seconds) on the order-service DB connection pool to prevent connections from being held indefinitely during checkout wait states.
recommendedActions.rationale: Code analyzer found the DB client uses a fixed pool with no adaptive backoff. Idle timeouts will force stale connections to be released back to the pool, breaking the saturation feedback loop.

recommendedActions.label: Enable circuit breaker on order-service DB dependency
recommendedActions.description: Activate a circuit breaker on the order-service's DB calls to stop attempting checkout when the pool is saturated, allowing the service to return 503s gracefully instead of blocking threads.
recommendedActions.rationale: With no backoff on checkout failures, every request blocks a thread waiting for a connection. A circuit breaker will shed load when the pool is exhausted, preventing thread-pool saturation and CPU spikes above 90%.

recommendedActions.label: Increase ECS task memory and CPU limits for order-service
recommendedActions.description: Raise the CPU and memory limits on the order-service task definition to provide adequate headroom for the current load, preventing OOM and CPU throttling.
recommendedActions.rationale: Infra inspector flagged CPU/memory as undersized for current load. Higher limits will allow tasks to process more requests concurrently and release connections faster.

recommendedActions.label: Add response cache for order-service read paths
recommendedActions.description: Introduce a caching layer (e.g., ElastiCache or application-level cache) for order-service read-heavy paths to reduce DB connection hold time and overall load.
recommendedActions.rationale: DB analyst confirmed pool sizing is the bottleneck, not query performance. Reducing the number of DB connections needed per request via caching will directly alleviate pool saturation.

recommendedActions.label: Review and optimize order-service DB queries
recommendedActions.description: Audit the order-service's SQL queries for opportunities to reduce connection hold time — add indexes, batch queries, or rewrite full-table scans that contribute to pool saturation.
recommendedActions.rationale: While no lock contention was found, long-running queries still hold connections. Optimizing queries will reduce the time each connection is held, increasing effective pool throughput.

evidence.content: Log analyst: order-service shows connection pool wait timeouts and thread-pool saturation under high CPU. Errors correlate with DB checkout failures.
Metric analyst: CPUUtilization sustained above 90% for order-service. Concurrent DB connections at pool max; queue depth rising.
Infra inspector: order-service running with 2 desired tasks. Task definition CPU/memory appear undersized for current load.
Code analyzer: order-service DB client uses a fixed pool size with no adaptive backoff on checkout failure. Recent code paths amplify connection churn under load.
Change detector: No recent deployments in the last 24h. Configuration unchanged. Issue appears load-driven rather than change-driven.
DB analyst: Connection waiters spiked while active connections hit the configured maximum. No lock contention on primary tables; pool sizing is the bottleneck.

customerExplanation.summary: The order-service, which handles customer orders, became unresponsive under load because its database connection pool was completely exhausted. When the pool runs out of connections, new requests pile up waiting, CPU spikes, and the service can no longer process anything — creating a cascading failure.
customerExplanation.impact: Customers experienced order processing delays, timeouts, and potential order failures. The service was unable to accept new requests while the pool was saturated.
customerExplanation.resolution: We are scaling the service from 2 to 6 tasks to immediately increase capacity, adding connection idle timeouts to release stale connections, and enabling a circuit breaker to gracefully shed load when the database is unreachable.
customerExplanation.eta: 30 minutes for immediate relief (scaling + circuit breaker); longer-term fixes (caching, query optimization) will be deployed in the next maintenance window.
